fast.summary: This paper presents an FPGA implementation of the IVF-PQ approximate nearest neighbor search algorithm that supports up to 1024-dimensional datasets, achieving 0.0071ms search latency at 94% recall. The design introduces a novel parallel Top-K architecture, mixed-precision optimization, and a reusable distance calculation module, outperforming state-of-the-art ASIC implementations by 4.5x in speed with 20% less energy.
fast.key_idea: By combining a parallelized Top-K module with precision-adjusted arithmetic and a flexible reusable distance calculation unit on an FPGA, IVF-PQ-based ANNS can achieve ultra-low latency and energy efficiency beyond even custom ASIC designs.
fast.why_it_matters: As LLM-powered edge applications (personal assistants, on-device RAG) increasingly rely on fast vector search, this work shows FPGAs can deliver sub-millisecond ANNS with low power draw, making high-quality semantic retrieval feasible on resource-constrained hardware without cloud dependency.
possible_use_cases: On-device RAG for personal AI assistants running on edge hardware
Low-latency recommendation and ad retrieval systems requiring real-time vector search
Energy-efficient semantic search in embedded or IoT environments where GPU/cloud access is unavailable

deep.method_breakdown: The paper implements the IVF-PQ (Inverted File with Product Quantization) algorithm on an FPGA (Xilinx Alveo U280), supporting up to 1024-dimensional vectors. The design features a novel Top-K module that handles multiple input streams in parallel for linear throughput scaling, a reusable Distance Calculation module shared across query stages (coarse quantization and PQ distance lookup), and reduced-precision arithmetic to improve performance with minimal recall loss. The full pipeline achieves 0.0071ms search latency at 94% recall with 19.80W power consumption.
deep.key_innovation: First FPGA-based ANNS hardware implementation supporting up to 1024-D datasets. The novel Top-K module architecture enables processing of multiple simultaneous input streams with linearly scaling throughput, unlike prior designs that bottleneck at the ranking stage. The flexible, reusable Distance Cal module is shared across coarse and fine quantization stages, reducing resource usage. Combined precision adjustment across pipeline stages yields performance gains without significant recall degradation.
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design choice is the multi-stream Top-K module. In ANNS pipelines, ranking candidate distances is a classic bottleneck because naive sorting is O(n log n) and hard to parallelize on fixed hardware. By designing a module that ingests multiple streams simultaneously and scales throughput linearly with stream count, the authors decouple the ranking stage from being the limiting factor, enabling the rest of the pipeline (distance computation, PQ lookup) to run at full utilization.
deep.limitations: The design is evaluated only on Xilinx Alveo U280, limiting generalizability to other FPGA families or embedded FPGAs for true edge deployment. IVF-PQ inherently trades recall for speed via quantization, and the 94% recall ceiling may be insufficient for high-precision retrieval tasks. The paper compares against ASIC baselines but not against GPU implementations at equivalent power budgets, making competitive positioning incomplete. Dynamic index updates (insertions/deletions) are not addressed, restricting applicability to static datasets.
interview_questions: How does IVF-PQ work, and what are the two main sources of approximation that enable its speed?
Why is the Top-K selection stage a bottleneck in ANNS pipelines, and how does this paper's architecture address it?
What are the tradeoffs of reducing numerical precision in an FPGA ANNS pipeline, and how would you decide where to apply it?
How does product quantization compress high-dimensional vectors, and what is the role of precomputed lookup tables in accelerating distance computation?
When would you choose an FPGA implementation of ANNS over a GPU-based solution, and what workload characteristics favor each?
interview_answers: IVF-PQ combines two approximations: (1) an inverted file index that clusters vectors into Voronoi cells so only a subset of cells (nearest to the query) are searched, reducing the candidate set; and (2) product quantization, which compresses residual vectors by splitting them into sub-vectors and quantizing each independently, enabling distance approximation via precomputed lookup tables rather than full floating-point computation.

Top-K selection requires maintaining a sorted or partially-sorted list of the best K candidates as distances stream in, which is inherently sequential if done naively. The paper addresses this by designing a module that accepts multiple distance values per clock cycle across parallel input streams, using a tournament-style or merge-sort hardware structure that scales throughput linearly with the number of streams, preventing the ranking stage from stalling the upstream distance computation pipeline.

Lower precision (e.g., INT8 vs FP32) reduces DSP usage, increases parallelism, and lowers power, but introduces quantization error that degrades recall. The optimal placement is in stages where errors are tolerant: PQ distance lookups and accumulation are good candidates since PQ already introduces approximation, while coarse centroid selection may need higher precision to avoid misrouting queries to wrong clusters. Empirical recall vs. precision sweeps on representative datasets guide the decision.

PQ splits a D-dimensional vector into M sub-vectors of D/M dimensions each, then independently quantizes each sub-space into one of K* centroids (typically 256). Each database vector is stored as M centroid indices. At query time, for each sub-space, distances from the query sub-vector to all K* centroids are precomputed into a lookup table (M tables of K* entries). The approximate distance to any database vector is then the sum of M table lookups — replacing expensive floating-point dot products with integer additions.

FPGAs excel when latency is the primary constraint, power budgets are tight (e.g., edge or datacenter TCO-sensitive), and the workload is steady-state with a fixed index (no frequent updates). GPUs are preferable when throughput (queries per second across large batches) matters more than single-query latency, when the index changes frequently, or when development velocity is critical since GPU ANNS libraries (FAISS, cuVS) are mature. For personal assistant edge scenarios with strict power limits, FPGAs are compelling; for large-scale search infrastructure, GPUs typically win on cost-efficiency.
